sections.heading: What Does Comparing Mean?
sections.content: Comparing two numbers means deciding which is bigger, which is smaller, or if they are the same. In the jungle, you compare groups all the time — which tree has more fruit? Which pile has fewer stones?

examples.problem: Which is more: 6 mangoes or 4 mangoes?

sections.heading: One-to-One Matching
sections.content: A great way to compare is to match items from one group to the other, one by one. The group that has items left over is the bigger group. If no items are left over, the groups are equal.

examples.solution: They are equal — both groups have 5
steps: Match each banana to a coconut

sections.heading: Using a Number Line
sections.content: On a number line, numbers get bigger as you move to the right. To compare two numbers, find them on the number line. The number further right is greater. The number further left is less.

common_pitfalls.mistake: Thinking a number with more digits is always bigger
common_pitfalls.correction: For numbers up to 20, count carefully — 9 is less than 10 even though 9 looks bigger
More = bigger number, less = smaller number
Always count each group first, then compare the totals
